story:
She did it again—took my afternoon snack. I’d lined my toy cars across the living room, maybe a little in front of the kitchen doorway, but I needed every inch for my city. The blueberry muffins baking in the oven smelled like heaven. Then Mom yelled my full name (never good). She’d kicked my cars, hurt her foot, messed up my whole layout, and declared: “No muffins for you!” When the timer dinged, she yanked the pan out with her mitts, shoved the muffins into the fridge, slammed the door, and stomped off. I stomped to my room, startling Booger, my cat—Mom and Dad think his name is Martin, but he told me ages ago it’s Booger. He curled around my leg, looked at me like a partner in crime, and I knew: we were taking those muffins back.

Every good heist starts by lulling the target. So I played it sweet. I apologized for the cars (even though Booger and I knew they belonged where they were). Mom hugged me. Perfect. The rest of the afternoon, I was extra helpful. I moved my cars, helped with dinner, asked Dad about his day. I didn’t say “muffin” once. Bedtime came. Phase two.

You have to know the route and the guards. That’s why I’d cleared the cars—I didn’t want to step on one in the dark and blow the whole operation. There’s a creaky floorboard outside my door. Avoid that. Mom and Dad are predictable: TV, loud whispering in their room, then Dad gets a glass of water. After that, they’re down for the night. I waited for Dad to get his water. Booger slinked to their door, listened, and came back with the all-clear. Showtime.

I slipped out, skirted the creaky board, and tiptoed to the kitchen with Booger. He took lookout near their door while I faced problem one: I couldn’t pull the fridge open. I could barely reach the handle. The step stool in the washroom was the only way, but I hadn’t mapped that route for hauling it quietly. Booger, like always, understood. He guided me across the room: step here, not there. At the washroom, problem two: carrying the stool quietly. We decided I’d move it a few inches at a time—set it down, move my foot, lift, repeat. It took forever, but the house stayed quiet.

Heists always need luck. Right as I reached the fridge, Booger screeched and Dad muttered, “Damn it, Martin,” shuffling out—no lights, thank goodness. Heart pounding, I hid in the shadows with the stool as Dad opened the fridge. My prize glowed like treasure. Then disaster: he grabbed a muffin—my muffin—and devoured it right there. I almost cried. He shut the door and went back to bed, leaving me with equal parts relief and rage. Booger curled in my lap until my breathing slowed. We might not get all the muffins now, but we could still win.

I squared up, set the stool, climbed, and pulled the fridge open. Cold air rushed my face. Booger pressed against the door like a furry wedge while I lifted the muffin tin; then I nudged the door closed. We slipped back to my room, dodged the creaky board, and latched the door. I know sneaking food isn’t exactly model behavior, but in my kid brain this was justice. Sitting cross-legged, Booger watching like a tiny sphinx, I ate the most delicious muffins of my life—cold, ill-gotten, and utterly victorious. Whatever came tomorrow, I was content.
